content: Is there a way to see the metadata for Zulip? Like the total member count, for example?
:gear:️ > Statistics.

Some interesting information there. Comparing the available timescales for the kinds of messages sent indicates a trend toward more discussions being done privately. If that continues, the public streams will just be the tip of the zulipberg. :thinking:
I wonder how much of the decrease of public posts is because fewer new people are showing up, and how much is because it's still (the tail end of) summer vacation?
UCR will not have a category theory seminar this fall, since I'm on sabbatical... so it's up to the MIT group to keep things lively with seminar discussions here on Zulip.
Wow, very interesting. I was completely oblivious of the popularity of private chats!

Jason Erbele said:

Some interesting information there. Comparing the available timescales for the kinds of messages sent indicates a trend toward more discussions being done privately. If that continues, the public streams will just be the tip of the zulipberg. :thinking:

This is true, what shall we do?
Why do we need to do anything?
Just let people use it how they want to use it

John Baez said:

I wonder how much of the decrease of public posts is because fewer new people are showing up, and how much is because it's still (the tail end of) summer vacation?

I think it's the opposite, given that peak activity was in July: people are getting back to work now that the new semester has started!

Yeah, I don't think a migration to a greater use of private chats is something that needs to be "fixed" per se. As long as the public streams are active, I don't think there is a necessarily a problem. More like, the public streams are providing an effective means for like-minded folks to find each other and form smaller collaborations. If that's what's happening, it's probably healthy.

I don't think the statistics are surprising. Private chats are used a lot, group private chats way less. Indeed, when I work with more than one person I prefer to use emails, so that messages are easily searchable. When I work with just one person I find mail to be uselessly formal, and I prefer to just exchange messages here. I guess this is a popular way of doing things?
